stories.headline: MCP just deleted the session model — and everything built on it breaks in 10 days
stories.hook: The spec that runs every AI agent's tools rewrote itself. If your app used sessions, it breaks on the 28th. Here's why that's actually the good news.
stories.signal: The MCP release candidate dated 28 July 2026 removes the initialize handshake and the Mcp-Session-Id header outright. Protocol version and client info now ride in _meta on every request — MCP is stateless at the protocol layer. SEP-2575 removes the handshake; SEP-2567 removes the session header.
stories.why: This is the change with the biggest operational impact in the spec's history. Anything built against the 2025-11-25 session model has hidden session dependencies that break — and the fix isn't cosmetic: requests must survive moving between server instances. Statelessness is what turns a metered endpoint from a demo into a rail.
stories.take: A stateless protocol layer quietly settles the “is this a real payment rail?” question. Session affinity is what kept metered agent endpoints from fanning out; remove it and verify-before-pay can scale horizontally like any other stateless HTTP service. The same release ships MCP Apps (sandboxed UI routed back through the same consent/audit path) and MCP's first Active→Deprecated→Removed policy — the quiet signal MCP is now run like enterprise infrastructure, and that the audit log is the product.
stories.counter: A spec landing days out isn't adoption. Tier-1 SDKs get a ten-week validation window, and every server built on the session model has to migrate under live traffic. And a “stateless core” that mints server-issued handles passed as tool args just relocated session state into app code — “stateless by 28 July” is the spec; “stateless in production” is a Q4 story.

sources.name: MCP 2026-07-28 Release Candidate — official spec blog

runit.label: Test whether your MCP server survives the 28th — 200 with no session header = you live; needs Mcp-Session-Id = you break:

stories.headline: The first ERC-8004 audit is in — and ~90% of “agents” on Base are Sybil
stories.hook: Everyone's racing to register on the agent-identity standard. The first audit says 9 in 10 are fake.

chart.title: Share of registered ERC-8004 agents with a valid, live registration
chart.note: The slivers are what's real. The rest is placeholder squatting — and on Base, 90.6% of registered agents showed coordinated fake-reviewer behavior.

stories.signal: The first empirical audit of every ERC-8004 agent across Ethereum, BSC and Base (revised 8 Jul) found only 3% / 4% / 15% (ETH/BSC/Base) exposed a valid registration with a live endpoint — the rest is placeholder squatting. Coordinated fake-reviewer behavior hit 90.6% on Base; reputation is “manipulable at minimal cost.” The EIP itself is still formally Draft.
stories.why: This is the empirical proof of the whole thesis: an on-chain registry standardizes where you write a claim, not whether it's true. A reputation number that's free to forge is theater — and the market is minting theater at scale, fast.
stories.take: The open seat is exactly what ERC-8004 leaves empty: interaction-grounded, stake-secured resolution — the Validation Registry hook that almost nobody is populating. A registry gives identity; it does not give you earned-not-declared standing with a payer key that can't tilt the verdict. That's the layer that makes the other 90% legible instead of ignorable.
stories.counter: Adoption is genuinely fast (six-figure registrations, ENS/EigenLayer/Graph integrations), a Draft standard getting gamed early is normal, and v2 explicitly targets payment-proof-backed feedback — the Sybil hole may partially close on its own. Don't mistake “early and gamed” for “doomed.”

runit.label: Do what the registry can not: check a counterparty is real before you pay it.

stories.headline: The payments industry just co-signed one agent rail — x402 goes to the Linux Foundation
stories.hook: Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, Google, AWS, Cloudflare and the stablecoin camp are now at one table. The protocol war ended before it started.
stories.signal: On 14 July, Coinbase's HTTP-402 “pay-in-the-request” protocol became a vendor-neutral Linux Foundation body with 40 members and a technical steering committee (Coinbase / Cloudflare / Stripe).
stories.why: When incumbents co-govern a standard this early, the protocol war ends before it starts — and the fight moves up-stack to identity, trust, and disputes. This is the strongest coalition any agent-payment standard has assembled.
stories.take: They agreed on how money moves and shipped zero agreement on how to verify the agent on the other end — that's our seat. A payments foundation with no conformance suite, no security profile, and no validation procedure is an open invitation for a signed-verification layer to sit on top. The rail is settled; the trust layer is not.
stories.counter: Foundation launches are governance theater until there's a ratified spec with conformance tests. “40 members” counts logos, not interop — and card networks joining standards bodies is defensive table-stakes, not a build commitment.

sources.name: Linux Foundation — x402 Foundation operational launch

runit.label: See the rail everyone just co-signed.

stories.headline: OpenAI splits GPT-5.6 into three agents — and halves the price of frontier reasoning
stories.hook: OpenAI didn't ship one model. It shipped three — and the orchestration now lives inside the model boundary.
stories.signal: GA on 9 July: Sol (frontier), Terra (~5.5-level intelligence at half the cost) and Luna (small, fast), plus a native ultra mode running four agents in parallel, Programmatic Tool Calling, and a multi-agent Responses API beta.
stories.why: Sol sets SOTA on the evals that gate autonomy (Terminal-Bench 2.1 88.8%, OSWorld 2.0 62.6%, BrowseComp 90.4%) at a fraction of the prior token cost. The shift is architectural: orchestration is moving inside the model boundary.
stories.take: When the model spawns and synthesizes sub-agents in one request, the trust question moves from “which model” to “what did the swarm actually do” — the audit surface fragments exactly where our signed-receipt seat lives. Cheaper frontier reasoning is the substrate; the moat was never the model, it's signed proof-of-execution on top of it.
stories.counter: Every headline number is OpenAI's own harness (latency and cost explicitly simulated), and rival indices still top some coding benchmarks — “best model” is index-selective. In-model orchestration also means fewer round-trips and fewer natural places to interpose a verifier.

runit.label: Read it from the source, not the recap.

stories.headline: Visa takes agentic checkout LIVE at real European merchants
stories.hook: Not a demo. Agents completed real purchases at real shops, under real Strong Customer Authentication.
stories.signal: At the Visa Payments Forum in Paris (2 Jul), agents completed real purchases at lastminute.com, Frasers, Cleverbridge and BrickDepot across 30+ issuers, secured by Visa Payment Passkeys and running on Visa's Trusted Agent Protocol + Agent Directory.
stories.why: The jump from controlled demo to agents transacting at independent checkouts under real SCA is the first at-scale proof agentic commerce can clear EU regulatory bars.
stories.take: Visa is quietly building the agent-identity/authorization layer (TAP + Agent Directory + Passkeys) that x402 lacks — which validates verification-as-product. But a closed Visa directory is a walled garden; the open, cross-network version of “know the agent before you settle” is the un-enclosable one, and that's the seat 0n1x plays.
stories.counter: “Live” here means a curated pilot cohort with hand-picked merchants. Volume, chargeback behavior, and non-Visa interop are all unproven.

runit.label: See the agent-identity layer Visa is quietly building.

stories.headline: Web Bot Auth: “sign the request, kill the IP allowlist” gets chartered at the IETF
stories.hook: Soon a website will cryptographically know it's really Anthropic or OpenAI knocking — not something wearing their user-agent.
stories.signal: Cloudflare's Web Bot Auth post refreshed 15 Jul; the IETF webbotauth working group is chartered with a standards-track milestone and a key-management BCP due August 2026. Built on HTTP Message Signatures (RFC 9421), origins confirm a caller's identity via keys at a .well-known directory.
stories.why: This is becoming the default agent↔web handshake at the HTTP layer — ending user-agent spoofing for signed crawlers.
stories.take: It answers “which vendor's bot is this,” not “is the agent's output trustworthy” — and the two compose. Owning the directory-of-keys posture is table-stakes; the durable value is what you attest after you know who's calling. A signed fetch header is the handshake; a signed verdict is the product.
stories.counter: It only proves operator identity for the big signed crawlers — nothing for the unsigned long tail — and the working group is still pre-RFC.

runit.label: Read the handshake that kills user-agent spoofing.
